```
# PRODUCT.md

# Product

## Register

brand

## Users

**Primary:** Potential clients and decision-makers who hire or partner for strategic and technical work, and who care about **ideas, framing, and judgment** as much as delivery.

**Context:** They arrive from search, referrals, or your network. They skim for credibility, clarity, and whether you think in outcomes, not only stack trivia. They may not identify as developers; they need to understand **why you** without wading through insider posture.

## Product Purpose

JoRap Notes is a **personal brand surface** at [jorap.com](https://www.jorap.com): it showcases how you think, what you stand for, and the quality of your work so that the right clients **recognize fit** and reach out with confidence.

Success looks like: visitors leave with a clear sense of **professional credibility**, **distinct point of view**, and **trust** that engaging you is a serious business choice, not a casual follow of a hobby blog.

## Brand Personality

**Voice:** Confident, direct, and composed. You explain complexity without performing it. Warmth is welcome; **whimsy and “fan service” to a tech in-group are not** the main register.

**Three-word personality:** **Professional, idea-led, credible.**

**Emotional goals:** Respect, clarity, and “I’d trust this person in a room with stakeholders,” not “this person’s online persona is a character.”

## Anti-references

- **Performative nerd / dev-bro energy:** Exhaustive flexing, in-jokes as identity, or a site that reads like a gaming stream overlay. You are not optimizing for peer applause at the expense of client comfort.
- **Generic SaaS landing sameness:** Interchangeable hero metrics, stock startup cadence, or template-y “thought leader” filler that could be anyone.
- **Chaotic personal brand:** Cute chaos, irony-as-personality, or visual noise that undermines the signal that you are **easy to work with** and **clear in communication**.

## Design Principles

1. **Ideas before opti
… (output truncated)
```

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Reading custom.css for graph-icon styles and reviewing page buttons.

Checking search, language switcher, theme switcher, and random note buttons. Current padding is inconsistent across breakpoints. Vertical padding is uniform for nav-links. Need to verify 44px minimum touch targets on mobile and tablet.

Found more inconsistencies: CTA buttons vary by breakpoint, dropdown links are too small, and filter buttons change padding at different widths. Search and exit buttons lack minimum sizes. Will define CSS custom properties for 44px touch targets and standard 8px vertical padding.

Clarified the requirement: each button and link must have equal top and bottom padding, and that same padding value must remain identical across desktop, tablet, and mobile breakpoints.

Removing breakpoint-specific padding overrides across all components. Buttons will use a fixed vertical padding everywhere, with min-height set for mobile and tablet touch targets. Will update buttons.css and navigation.css to enforce this consistency.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Reviewing partials and base.css to locate where to add touch target tokens.

Planning unified CSS custom properties for touch targets and button padding. Considering 2.75rem minimum height, 0.5rem vertical padding for standard buttons, and 0.4375rem for small buttons.

Filter pills will use 0.5rem 0.75rem padding everywhere with a 2.75rem minimum height on touch devices. Notes-graph-btn will switch to 0.5rem 0.65rem symmetric padding with the same minimum height. Nav-links will keep 0.75rem vertical padding on mobile.

Nav-link vertical padding is already consistent across breakpoints. Nav-dropdown-link needs larger vertical padding and a minimum height. Nav-item CTA buttons will use uniform py-2 padding everywhere. The header search button needs a minimum height and width with centered flex alignment.

The `[data-notes-review-exit]` element needs symmetric vertical padding and a minimum height because it is styled as a text link rather than a button. The implementation plan now includes three files: base.css for touch target tokens, buttons.css for inline-flex, centered alignment, touch-action, and responsive minimum heights, and navigation.css for dropdown links, CTA buttons, and nav-link touch targets.

Custom.css will unify filter and button paddings while removing breakpoint-specific overrides. Minimum height will apply only within the max-xl media query, since desktop does not need 44px touch targets. Vertical padding must remain identical across all breakpoints.

Drafting the concrete CSS for buttons. Standard `.btn` elements will use `inline-flex`, `items-center`, and `justify-center` with `touch-action: manipulation` and a subtle accent tap highlight. Both `.btn` and `.btn-sm` will share identical 0.5rem vertical padding. A `@media (max-width: 1279.98px)` query will enforce a 2.75rem minimum height on both button variants for touch devices.

Raising `.btn-sm` vertical padding to 0.5rem is a deliberate design choice to unify touch targets across breakpoints. `.nav-link` will use symmetric 0.75rem padding everywhere, switching only horizontal spacing on desktop. `.nav-dropdown-link` is being restyled with the same semantic classes.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I now have a clear picture. I will implement a consolidated solution to standardize button and link padding across all devices.

Six files need updates. Buttons.css gets core touch padding. Navigation.css fixes nav inconsistencies. Custom.css unifies notes filters and graph buttons. Header.html needs a larger search button touch target. The theme switcher knob also needs a minimum touch area.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="read_file_v2"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **read_file_v2** • Read file: /home/jorap/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com/themes/jorap/assets/css/base.css</summary>

</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (default)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="read_file_v2"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **read_file_v2** • Read file: /home/jorap/Documents/GitHub/jorap.com/themes/jorap/assets/css/main.css</summary>

</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Custom.css loads last, so its styles override the theme.

Global button and navigation styles belong in the theme's buttons.css and navigation.css. Custom.css should only hold site-specific overrides. I'll remove redundant mobile padding from custom.css and unify filter styles. The header template needs updating too. Generated theme CSS likely comes from those theme files.

Base.css will define shared touch-target variables like `--touch-target-min` and `--btn-py`. Theme-level buttons.css and navigation.css will hold the global padding rules. Custom.css will keep only site-specific overrides.

The theme's buttons.css now applies vertical padding via `--btn-py` and `--btn-py-sm`, adds `touch-action: manipulation`, and sets a minimum height of `--touch-target-min` on small screens. The `.nav-link` selector is next.

The `.nav-link` rule now uses flex alignment, horizontal padding, and `touch-action: manipulation`. The `.nav-dropdown-link` rule applies the same vertical padding and enforces a minimum height. Next is the CTA button variant inside `.nav-item--cta`.

The CTA button variant inherits vertical padding from `.btn` instead of overriding it. `.nav-link` keeps its `block` display with explicit symmetric padding. The dropdown link’s minimum height is acceptable on desktop for consistency. The header search button in `layouts/_partials/essentials/header.html` still needs updating.

The header search button now uses `min-h-11` for the 2.75rem touch target. The language switcher gets `min-h-11`, symmetric padding, and `touch-manipulation`. The theme switcher also receives `min-h-11`. These classes replace the previous padding-only overrides.

Filter buttons now use `inline-flex` with centered alignment, `var(--filter-py)` vertical padding, and `min-height: var(--touch-target-min)`. Graph buttons get `min-height` and `min-width` set to the touch target size. The graph view container also pulls from the same `--touch-target-min` variable.

The mobile media query will be stripped down to sticky toolbar behavior, hiding filter suffixes, and graph control insets. The notes-graph-search input will use a fixed 1rem font size to prevent iOS zoom, paired with a 0.5rem by 0.75rem padding and a 2.75rem minimum height.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Applying min-height to all nav-links would incorrectly affect the "View all posts" link. A more targeted selector is needed.

Scope touch targets to `.navbar-nav .nav-link` only. Home-writing's `p-3` padding is too large for inline links.

Home-writing links keep their existing padding. The minimum height and touch-action rules apply only to `.navbar-nav .nav-link`.

Pre-existing padding on home-writing links is out of scope. Focus stays on buttons and clearly interactive controls. Dropdown links will be scoped to `.nav-dropdown-list .nav-dropdown-link`.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Implementing consistent symmetric vertical padding and 44px touch targets across buttons, nav, and notes controls.

The accordion header already has symmetric vertical padding from vendor CSS.

Adding vertical padding to the accordion header risks doubling the existing 1rem vendor padding unless the override explicitly overrides it. The current module override only adds min-height, which is safe.

</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (default)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The tab navigation item uses vendor padding of 0.5rem 1rem with !important.

The module override applies `px-8` from `@apply`, which may conflict with the vendor's `!important` padding shorthand. The vendor's padding still overrides the custom `padding-top` and `padding-bottom` values unless `!important` is added.

The vendor padding already equals the custom vertical padding, so the touch target remains intact. The minimum height is applied only on mobile and tablet, while desktop buttons keep their standard padding. This satisfies the requirement for consistent padding across breakpoints.
</details></think>

_**Agent (default)**_

Audited buttons and interactive links across the site and standardized them around shared touch-target tokens.

### What changed

**Design tokens** (`base.css`)
- Added `--touch-target-min: 2.75rem` (44px), `--btn-py: 0.5rem`, `--nav-link-py: 0.75rem`, and `--filter-py: 0.5rem` so padding stays symmetric and consistent across breakpoints.

**Buttons** (`buttons.css`)
- Switched to `inline-flex` with equal top/bottom padding from `--btn-py`
- `.btn-sm` now uses the same vertical padding as `.btn` (was `py-1.5`, which was smaller and inconsistent)
- On tablet/mobile (below 1280px), buttons get `min-height: 44px` for touch without changing padding at desktop

**Navigation** (`navigation.css`)
- Nav links: symmetric vertical padding at all breakpoints (removed the old `py-2.5` mobile / `py-2` desktop split on the CTA)
- Dropdown links: bumped from `py-1` to `--btn-py` with 44px min-height
- Header search, language select, and theme toggle: 44px touch targets via new utility classes

**Notes UI** (`custom.css`)
- Filter pills, graph controls, zoom buttons, and search input now share the same symmetric padding everywhere
- Removed breakpoint-only padding overrides that made mobile/tablet differ from desktop
- Graph overlay icons use 44px at all sizes (were 28px on desktop)

**Other interactive elements**
- Pagination: new `.pagination-control` class with consistent padding and 44px targets
- Back-to-top: `h-10` → `h-11` (40px → 44px)
- Tab nav items: 44px min-height with symmetric padding

**Templates**
- Updated header partials for search/language/theme controls

Theme CSS was rebuilt with `node scripts/themeGenerator.js`. If you have `hugo server` running, refresh to see the changes. The main areas to spot-check: header nav (mobile menu), notes toolbar/filters, graph controls, and pagination.
